column1: Certainly! Here’s a concise timeline table summarizing the key developments in the belief in the Devil (Satan) from 950 BCE to 70 CE, based on the content you provided. The table includes references, citations, and relevant quotes.
Timeline Table: The Development of Belief in the Devil (950 BCE – 70 CE)

column2:
- Israelite religion (Yahwism) was polytheistic, with a pantheon of gods.
- No concept of Satan as Yahweh's opponent.
- Angels not named; lower-tier beings.
- Disasters attributed to Yahweh or lesser gods.
column3:
- “Yahwism...was, like other Northwest Semitic religions, polytheistic” (Niehr 1995:71-72; Smith 2004:101-110).
- “The place names and the personal names preserved from Iron Age I reveal the designations of at least five deities...” (Hess 2007:242).
- Psalm 82:1-2, Psalm 89:6-8 (court of gods).
- “None of the angels are mentioned by name during this period...”

column2:
- Hezekiah and Josiah attempt monolatric/henotheistic reforms.
- Move towards worship of Yahweh alone.
- Polytheistic practice persists.
column3:
- “This first monolatric reform was not very successful...” (Albertz 2009:383).
- “Israel’s official religion changed from being polytheistic into one that can be classified as monolatric or henotheistic.” (Becking 2001:192).

column2:
- Destruction of Jerusalem and Temple.
- Collapse of polytheistic pantheon; shift towards monotheism.
- Yahweh seen as responsible for all events.
- Suffering prompts theological crisis.
column3:
- “The Babylonian conquest...had an enormous impact on the Judean belief system. Their symbolic world suddenly collapsed.”
- Lamentations 5:20-22 (trauma and questioning God).
- “The Deuteronomists read their perspective into the earlier history of Israel, creating the impression that Israel’s religion was monotheistic from the start.”

column2:
- Emergence of Satan as a heavenly being in texts (Zechariah, Job, 1 Chronicles).
- Satan not yet Yahweh’s opponent; a member of the divine court.
- Increased angelology; angels named.
- Development of dualistic thinking under Hellenistic influence.
- Suffering explained via Satan/Devil/Belial/Lucifer.
column3:
- “There are only three books in the Old Testament in which the noun is used to refer exclusively to a heavenly being: Zechariah 3:1-2; Job 1:6-12; 2:1-7:1 Chronicles 21:1.”
- “None of these books characterises ‘Satan’ as Yahweh’s opponent; he is merely a member of the heavenly court.”
- “Believers...resorted to belief in Satan...as a way of making sense of their world.”

column2:
- Satan/Devil becomes central to Christian theology.
- Used to explain evil, temptation, and suffering.
- Christianity “cannot do without this character.”
column3:
- “True Christianity is stuck with the Devil...” (Carl Braaten, in Oldridge 2012:92).
- “The devil has been part of the fabric of European life since the Middle Ages, and has accompanied all its major changes” (Muchembled 2003:1).

column1: “The devil has been part of the fabric of European life since the Middle Ages, and has accompanied all its major changes.” (Muchembled 2003:1)
“Monotheism is a ‘game’ with no more than two players, God and human beings. The origin of evil and human suffering cannot be sought outside these two players.” (Cees den Heyer 1998:29)
On Christianity’s Need for the Devil:
“True Christianity is stuck with the Devil...” (Carl Braaten, in Oldridge 2012:92)
Muchembled, R. (2003). A History of the Devil: From the Middle Ages to the Present.
Loader, J. (1984). “Liefs na die duiwel met ou Niek!”
